coredata.dc:description: Fluoride ion is a crucial micronutrient in living organisms which assist in growth, development and bone metabolism, but excess accumulation of this ion can cause fluorosis, arthritis, bone damage, osteoporosis, muscular damage, fatigue, joint-related problems, etc. Sometimes adverse effects are also seen with the overdose of this ion like damage of heart, arteries, kidney, liver and several other delicate parts of a living organism. Hence, selective and sensitive detection of this ion by colorimetric and fluorimetric method have gained huge interest due to the simple technique, cost effectiveness and high selectivity. Literature survey shows a variety of fluoride ion sensors based on organic molecules, metal nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots etc., which are not completely soluble in aqueous solution and also toxic in nature. Thus detection of F- in water media and biological systems by using these probes is not favorable. In this regard carbon dots (CDs) have proved itself as a promising and novel material due to its easy and low cost preparation, high water solubility, less toxicity and moreover good biocompatibility. Consequently, special emphasis has been given to the CDs-based green sensors for the selective and sensitive detection of F- ions in aqueous environments, real samples and biological systems through different fluorimetric methods and their working principles have been discussed. Most interestingly, various ready-to-use systems and mobile application (APP) based/smartphone assisted systems have also been developed for F- ions sensing, which are user friendly, portable and very much attractive.

head.ce:title: Detection of fluoride ion by carbon dots-based fluorescent probes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluoride ion is a crucial micronutrient in living organisms which assist in growth, development and bone metabolism, but excess accumulation of this ion can cause fluorosis, arthritis, bone damage, osteoporosis, muscular damage, fatigue, joint-related problems, etc. Sometimes adverse effects are also seen with the overdose of this ion like damage of heart, arteries, kidney, liver and several other delicate parts of a living organism. Hence, selective and sensitive detection of this ion by colorimetric and fluorimetric method have gained huge interest due to the simple technique, cost effectiveness and high selectivity. Literature survey shows a variety of fluoride ion sensors based on organic molecules, metal nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots etc., which are not completely soluble in aqueous solution and also toxic in nature. Thus detection of F- in water media and biological systems by using these probes is not favorable. In this regard carbon dots (CDs) have proved itself as a promising and novel material due to its easy and low cost preparation, high water solubility, less toxicity and moreover good biocompatibility. Consequently, special emphasis has been given to the CDs-based green sensors for the selective and sensitive detection of F- ions in aqueous environments, real samples and biological systems through different fluorimetric methods and their working principles have been discussed. Most interestingly, various ready-to-use systems and mobile application (APP) based/smartphone assisted systems have also been developed for F- ions sensing, which are user friendly, portable and very much attractive.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data have been used from the literature for which reference numbers are shown in the article. Permission for using the figures has been taken from the publishers.

ce:section.ce:para: Ready-to-use devices and application (APP) based/smartphone assisted systems based on carbon dots are becoming more and more popular in recent times in various areas such as chemical industry, biotechnology and medicine [1–4]. With this growing interest scientists round the world have also engaged to develop such readymade systems which are applicable for the selective and sensitive detection of important bio-analytes [5–7]. Fluoride ion (F-) is a biologically significant analyte, which is essential for maintaining healthy teeth and skeleton. F- is often found in groundwater in many regions in the earth and it is also found in osteoporosis drugs and toothpaste (low concentration range) [8], but excess accumulation of this ion has several adverse effects in human body and also has potential health risk [9]. Surplus F- ion concentration can causes permanent damage of teeth, bones and kidney [10]. Exposure to high level of F- for a long time can have an negative impact on children's IQ [11]. Maximum acceptable levels of F- ion in drinking water has been set by World Health Organization (WHO), which should not exceed 1.5 mg l-1 (79.0 μM) [12]. Hence, it is highly significant to develop simple straightforward method for sensitive and selective detection of F- in water media and biological systems for the minimization of health risk.

Different techniques for the determination of F- by using several probes are reported in literature which mainly includes colorimetry, fluorescence sensing, electrode analysis and nuclear magnetic resonance method [13–16]. Among them fluorescence method is most popular and important due to the low operational cost, non-invasive methodology, molecular and cellular imaging, simple operational process, specificity, rapidity and very low level detection of analytes [17]. Thus, recognition of in vivo and in vitro species with high sensitivity is being done by fluorescence spectroscopy in a very simple manner. Most importantly, in fluorescence method various systems can be constructed as per requirement for the successful detection of the analytes involving different principles like on-off-on, inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), Photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Aggregation-induced emission (AIE), host-guest interaction etc.

As F- is widely found in drinking water in many regions round the world, so, detection of this ion in aqueous environment is very important. Sensing of F- by organic molecules (like azo, urea and Benzothiazole) based systems and semiconductor quantum dots based probes are reported [18,19], but solubility of these probes in aqueous medium is very less and these are found to be toxic in nature. Interestingly, carbon dots being a water soluble system along with low toxicity, facile surface modification, robust chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence, high resistance to photobleaching and good biocompatibility has become more and more promising material for the low level sensing of bioanalytes in aqueous media, living cell, food and environmental samples [20–23]. The excellent electronic properties of CDs as electron donors and acceptors causing electrochemical luminescence and chemiluminescence endow them with large potentials in optoelectronics [24,25], biosensing [26–31], bioimaging [32,33], drug delivery [34,35] etc.

Direct sensing of F- by using CDs in fluorescence method are very rare in literature, Rather, different composite materials have been found where CDs are attached with metal ions, organic molecules, nanoparticles to construct hybrid systems which can detect F- very effectively by different fluorescence techniques like on-off-on, IFE, AIE etc. Metal ions such as Fe3+, Eu3+, Al3+ have been observed to be incorporated with the CDs where the probes are seen to function with off-on-on mechanism to detect F-. In some cases organic molecules like curcumin, morin etc., have been coupled to CDs to make hybrid systems which have been found to detect F- by IFE mechanism. Examples of other nanoparticles based CDs like silica, gold nanoparticles, and coordination compound incorporated CDs are also found which can detect F- through AIE strategy, FRET etc.

Moreover, in recent time not only simple fluorescence based probes are being made but in many cases ready-to-use systems like agarose gel based sensor, 36,37] test paper strip [38], mobile application (APP)/smartphone assisted systems [39–42] have been developed by the researchers which are getting very much popular due to their good sensitivity and selectivity towards various analytes and also portability for use. This report focuses on the mechanisms of some CDs-based sensors (Table 1 ) as well as ready-to-use systems for the successful detection of fluoride ions in aqueous environment and various real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the mechanism of fluoride ion sensing by CDs-based systems
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence of the CDs can be quenched by the addition of various analytes and this can be the basis for the successful detection of different analytes. Depending on the diverse fluorescence mechanism/strategy the CDs can be used as a fruitful senor to detect the analytes. The quenching mechanisms of CDs in detecting anlytes includes static quenching, dynamic quenching, FRET, PET, IFE etc.

Literature survey on the fluoride ion detection by CDs exhibits that direct sensing of this ion by CDs is very rarely reported. Thus development of such hybrid/composite systems has been done by the researchers in which basic component is CDs and the hybrid systems can selectively and sensitively detect F- in aqueous media and real samples. Mechanistic study of these probes shows that they can function with different fluorimetric methods like on-off-on, IFE, AIEE, FRET, PET etc. Colorimetric method in some cases has also been observed to detect the F- concentration with the help of necked eye. Researchers have also developed attractive ready-to-use systems and mobile application (APP)/smartphone assisted platforms for F- ion detection which are user friendly, portable and applicable for immediate use.

The process of fluoride ion sensing by CDs-based systems is discussed in this report. The simple/hybrid fluorescence probes used for F- detection and their mechanisms are shown in Table 1 and briefly discussed here. It has been foundn that metal ions such as Fe3+, Eu3+, Al3+ have been incorporated with the CDs which are observed to be functioned as a probe involving off-on-on mechanism to detect F-. In some cases organic molecules like curcumin, morin etc., have been added to CDs to make hybrid systems which are found to act via IFE mechanism. Examples of other nanoparticle based CDs like silica, gold nanoparticles, and also coordination compound incorporated CDs are found which can detect F- through fluorescence methods like aggregation-induced enhanced emission, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Scheme 1 ) etc.

Photoluminescence of CDs can either be quenched or enhanced by the addition of analytes and the change in fluorescence intensity can be used to detect the specific system. In on-off-on strategy first the fluorescence of the CDs is being quenched by adding quencher and then addition of the analyte to be detected is added to regenerate the fluorescence of the CDs [43,44]. Here, in this report different metal ions like Al3+, Fe3+, Eu3+ etc. and metal complexes have been used as quencher to turn off the fluorescence of the CDs.

If the absorption spectrum of quencher overlapped with the excitation or emission spectra of CDs in the detection system then IFE occurs. This mechanism of CDs is different from static and dynamic quenching. In this process modification of CDs is not necessary as well as it does not need the complicated instruments or vast calculations. Thus, it is less costly and complicated. Various analytes can be detected through this quenching mechanism of CDs [43,45].

Aggregation or crystallization of the fluorophore causes AIEE. In this process non-luminescent molecules in solutions are induced to exhibit very good emission upon aggregation [43,46]. If free-rotating groups in the organic compounds rotate in solution then they are activated instead of emitting light. When these substances form an aggregate to produce fluorescence, this rotation is constrained.

FRET is observed when the emission spectrum of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the quencher, it occurs between CDs in the excited state and quencher in the ground state [43,47]. This is an electrodynamic phenomenon and with the help of classical physics it can be explained. Long range dipole-dipole interactions between CDs and quencher are responsible for FRET mechanism. It can be seen that the distance between the CDs and quencher is in the range of 10 Å-100 Å.

In PET, electron transfer occurs between the CDs (electron donor or electron receptor) and the quencher (electron receptor or electron donor) [43,48]. Here, a complex has been formed between the electron donor and the electron receptor that can return to the ground state without emission of a photon. In oxidative PET, CDs act as an electron donor while in the reductive PET CDs act as an electron receptor and receive electron from the electron donor. Energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of quencher and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) of CDs is the driving force for reductive electron transfer. On the other hand in oxidative electron transfer, the energy gap between the LUMO of the CDs and the LUMO of the quencher plays an important role.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different types of CDs-based system used in F- detection
ce:section.ce:para: Doping/surface modification in CDs enables them for various potential applications. In several cases it has been observed that doped CDs to exhibit better optoelectronic properties than the undoped CDs [49,50]. Thus, as per need doping/surface modification in CDs can be done to get target oriented properties. Here, in thisn present endeavor various CDs-based fluorescent probes are reported where some CDs were synthesized from biomass/natural products, sometimes nonmetal doped CDs have been synthesized and coupled with other system to make hybrid probes. CDs are coupled with different metal ions and these CDs-Mn+ systems have been applied in successful F- detection. Composite systems where CDs are coupled with different organic molecule/coordination complex/nanoparticles have also been reported for effective F- detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Undoped and biomass derived CDs-based system
ce:section.ce:para: Basu et al. reported a simple ‘on-off-on’ approach for the sensitive and selective detection of F- in aqueous solution by using CDs [51]. A green strategy was followed to synthesize these CDs from easily available precursor such as Sabudana (Tapioca Sago). First of all the fluorescence of the CDs was turned off by introducing Fe3+ ion, which quenched the PL intensity of the CDs through charge transfer process. A parallel study was done by using Fe3+and Cu2+ to quench the PL intensity of CDs. Fe3+ ions was found to be more pronounced than Cu2+. To check the selectivity of the system towards the anion, different negatively charged ions which are present in water like F-, Cl-, NO 3- and SO4 2- were introduced to the CDs-Fe3+ system. The steady state fluorescence result suggested that the chemo-sensor is highly selective towards F- ion. It has been noticed that added F- ions are able to remove the Fe3+ from the surface of the CDs. Thus the PL intensity is restored to the "turn on" state (Fig. 1 ). Addition of F- to the CDs-Fe3+ system forms FeF3 complex, which is quite stable (insoluble) in aqueous solution. Interestingly, agarose gel based ready-to-use off/on type chemo-sensor device [Fig. 2 (i)] was also developed for practical application which has been found to be very effective for the spontaneous detection of F- ion in water. For further confirmation confocal microscopy was performed and similar result was obtained [Fig. 2(ii)].

Boruah et al. reported the synthesis of CDs from different biomass waste materials such as sugarcane bagasse (SCB), garlic peels (GP), and taro peels (TP) by employing ultrasonic-assisted wet-chemical-oxidation method [52], which is a green and eco-friendly approach. A 1:1 molar ratio of Eu3+ solution and taro peels derived CDs (T-CDs) was added to generate a new nanoprobe CDs-Eu3+. It has been observed that in presence of 100 ppm Eu3+ concentration, the emission intensity of the CDs quenched drastically due to the presence of sufficient electron donor groups. Absorption study suggests the binding of Eu3+ with the carboxylic groups in the surface of T-CDs. XPS study and FESEM image also reveal the same binding between Eu3+ and T-CDs and formation of T-CDs/Eu3+ nanoprobe leading to a faster fluorescence quenching. Addition of fluoride ions regenerate the fluorescence intensity of T-CDs. Carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups present in the surface of CDs is believed to be an excellent detector for F- ions. Moreover, being a hard acid Eu3+ is found to have strong affinity towards hard base like F- in water. EDS and FE-SEM images confirmed that upon addition of fluoride ions, EuF3 is formed gradually and thereby amount of Eu3+ decreases on the surface of CDs. Thus, switch-off PL of T-CDs (upon addition of Eu3+) has been found to switch-on upon addition of F- ion. Authors have demanded that this work can be a new opportunity of biomass waste management for its sustainable utilization as well as detection of F- in water samples.

Raveendran et al. reported microwave assisted synthesis of CDs by using arrowroot powder as starting material [53]. To check the sensing capacity of the as synthesized fluorescence probe, different anions like acetate, bromide, sulfate; carbonate, fluoride, chloride, nitrate and iodide were added to the CDs solutions. A notable influence on the intensity of CDs has been observed only with the addition of F- ions. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution is seen to be significantly quenched by the F- ions, which makes the probe very selective towards F- ions. It has been seen that increasing concentration of fluoride solution decreases the fluorescence of CDs sequentially. A good linear relationship has been observed between relative quenching efficiency (F0/F) and concentration of F-. The calculated limit of detection is 521 nM by using this probe. The probe is not only used to detect F- ions but also the combination of CDs and F-, i.e. CDs-F- system has been employed to detect Al3+ ion. Upon addition of Al3+ ions to the CDs-F- system, the fluorescence intensity is found to be recovered again. Thus a commendable selectivity is observed in fluorescence restoration in the presence of Al3+ ions. So, this probe can be beneficial for dual analytes recognition i.e. F- and Al3+ in fluorescence off-on fashion.

Colorimetric and fluorimetric dual mode detection of F- was investigated by designing fluorescent CDs derived from wheat straw by a green hydrothermal method [54]. The CDs have been found to be soluble in water with good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. The blue fluorescent CDs selectively detect F- ion, which has been checked by the addition of different anions with the CDs solution. With the increase of F- ion concentration the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is found to be decreased significantly, i.e. the intensity is quenched by F- ions. The quenching mechanism may be explained by considering the electron or energy transfer of CDs, which arises from strong interaction between surface functional groups (like carboxyl and hydroxyl) of CDs and F-. This affinity accelerates the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through the electron or charge transfer. A good linear relationship of fluorescence intensity versus F- ion concentration is seen in the range of 0–1.5 mM. The detection limit has been estimated to be 49 μM. Not only the present system was useful to detect F- by fluorescence mode but also it can identify F- in a necked eye colorimetric method. Many organic probes like azo, urea and benzothiazole are reported to be utilized as colorimetric probe for F- detection. This can be explained by taking into the account that a hydrogen bond is being formed between the receptor and F- ions (Fig. 3 ). This fact motivated them to make a colorimetric detection study of F- with the as prepared CDs containing different functional groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl etc. Interestingly, a visual color change from light yellow to red is seen (Fig. 3) by the naked eye when CDs solution has been incubated with F-. This result indicates that the CDs can be used as a visual colorimetric probe for fast F- ions sensing in aqueous media. The observed color change is described as the change in electronic structure due to formation of strong hydrogen bonding between carboxyl/hydroxyl group and strongly electronegative F-. Moreover, Low cytotoxicity of the CDs encouraged them to apply it for intracellular sensing of F-.

A novel reusable fluorescence probe was constructed by Sahu et al. for the detection and removal of F- from aqueous media using external magnetic field. Fluorescent CDs (synthesized from orange juice) has been used as fluorophore, whereas magnetically separable nickel ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) complex bound-silica coated magnetite nanoparticle (Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA-Ni) is used as F- ion receptor [55]. CDs are found to exhibit a strong fluorescence whereas addition of Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA-Ni to the aqueous CDs solution quenches the PL of CDs. This may be due to the binding of CDs to the magnetic receptor. Surprisingly, gradual addition of F- resumes the fluorescence intensity. Attachment of F- ions to the receptor causes removal of CDs from the binding site of the receptor. High resolution F1s XPS spectrum confirms the binding of F- with the receptor. Different anions have been introduced to the probe to check the selectivity of the system, and high selectivity towards F- is observed. The limit of detection is estimated to be 0.06 μM with a good linear response range of 1–20 μM. This probe can be used for the detection of F- in tap water. Easy, fast and assessable separation of the fluoride bound sensor from the analytic solution can be done by the nanosensor which offers quantification of F- ion. This probe shows a good cell permeability and low cytotoxicity, which can be used for sensing and imaging of F- in living cells.

A green, economical and selective probe was assembled by Singhal et al. which are found to be employed for the fluoride ion detection in real environmental samples [56]. First of all carboxylic acid functionalized water soluble CDs were synthesized by using citric acid and its photoluminescence behavior was checked by introducing Eu3+ion. A quenching in fluorescence intensity (switch-off) has been noticed. This can be explained by considering the electron transfer from CDs to Eu3+ and aggregate formation by the CDs. HRTEM and time resolved emission study confirm this fact. Binding between the Eu3+ and surface -COOH group is confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Zeta potential studies. Different competing ions have been introduced to the CDs-Eu3+ system to check the selectivity of the probe. Interestingly, a significant fluorescence enhancement is noticed when F- ions are added to the probe compared to the other analytes. This fact proves that CDs become free and regains its PL (switch-on) in presence of F- ions (Fig. 4 ). This may be due to the removal of Eu3+ from the surface of CDs and formation of stable EuF3 complex with high lattice energy. The complex formation (EuF3) has also been confirmed by HRTEM and X-ray diffraction measurements (Fig. 4). The working range of probe is found to be 1–25 ppm. Detection of F- ions in toothpaste sample by this probe has also been tested successfully.

Li et al. reported the synthesis and application of a new fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ and F- simultaneously [57]. CDs were obtained by the hydrothermal pyrolysis of salicylic acid. Different metal ions such as Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K +, Al3+, Na+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Ag+ have been introduced to the CDs solution and amusingly found that only Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in maximum extent. The system is also sensitive towards Fe3+ with a good linearity in concentration range of 10–300 µM. Detection limit is calculated to be 52 nM. Authors have suggested that the quenching in PL of CDs may be due to the complex formation between carboxyl/hydroxyl groups present on the surface of CDs and Fe3+. Different experimental results suggested a static quenching to be occurred between Fe3+ and CDs. Being a hard acid Fe3+ is found to combine with the oxygen-containing functional groups in CDs to produce nonfluorescent complexes which quenches the fluorescence of CDs. Surprisingly, the quenching PL of CDs-Fe3+ system is seen to be restored only by F-, although several anions are tested with the probe. This result has suggested the selectivity of CDs-Fe3+ system towards F- ions. This fact can be explained by taking into the account that detachment of Fe3+ ions takes place from the surface of CDs, which combines to F- to form a stable ionic compound (FeF3). Thus a fluorescence on-off-on mechanism has been observed for this nanoprobe. A good linear relationship is observed over the concentration range of 0.1–200 µM. Limit of detection is estimated to be 8.5 nM. Less toxicity and very good biocompatibility encouraged the authors to apply the probe for imaging Fe3+ and F- in living cell and Escherichia coli. Additionally, an “AND” logic gate has also constructed at molecular level.

Shamsipur et al. reported a CDs-based fluorescent probe which can detect F- ions in aqueous media, toothpaste and river water with a good recovery values [58]. CDs were synthesized from the ionic liquid N-octylpyridinum hexafluorophosphate (OPPF6) followed by microwave synthesis. An organic receptor 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA) was added to CDs to make CDs/MPBA hybrid system. Quenching in the fluorescence intensity of CDs is noticed with the gradual addition of F- ions. Boronic acid and SH centers present in the receptor is found to have covalent reaction with F- and make the signal quenching more efficient. A Linear relationship over 0–26.7 mM concentration of F- with a LOD value of 1.1 × 10–4 M is observed with the present probe. Fluoride level in real sample like toothpaste has also been tested successfully by this method.

A novel hybrid nanosystem was constructed by Zhang and coworkers [59], in which carbon dots are coupled with curcumin and used for sensitive and selective sensing probe for F- ions. Hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine and selenourea in hydrochloric acid resulted the Se, N co-doped CDs which was found to exhibit an excitation-dependent untypical emission with two intensive bands at blue and yellow colors. Authors have explained that blue emission is associated to the core structures, whereas yellow emission generates from the surface states. Curcumin is seen to exihibit a broad band at 532 nm. It is pertinent to mention here that an overlap is observed with both the yellow emission and excitation peaks of CDs and absorption band of curcumin suggesting suppression in the yellow fluorescence of CDs due to inner filter effect (IFE). In presence of F- a complex formation (curcumin-F-) occurs which leads to the blue shift of the absorption band from 532 to 430 nm. Thus IFE reduces, and yellow fluorescence of CDs recovers. Detection limit of this probe towards F- was measured to be 0.39 μM. This study encouraged the authors to measure F- in tap water and milk samples i.e. in real samples with relative standard deviations less than 7.9 %.

Combined N-doped CDs and morin-Al3+based integrated sensing platform was designed by Hao and coworkers [60]. This system was found to be act as ratiometric optical dual-mode sensor for F- ions. Hydrothermal treatment of a mixture of o-Phenylenediamine and citric acid produced the CDs. Morin solution and Al3+ solution were added to the CDs solution to make the fluorescence probe based on IFE. NCDs/morin-Al3+ system is found to exhibit dual emission wavelengths. An inner filter effect has been observed between NCDs and morin-Al3+. Addition of F- ions to this probe forms Al3+-F- complex. Thus an inhibition of IFE results in the decrease in morin-Al3+fluorescence and increase in NCDs fluorescence (Fig. 5 ). Stronger interaction between Al3+ and F- than that of Al3+ and morin is the key factor for the construction of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor NCDs/morin-Al3+. This system can be used for the detection of F- up to a wide range (0.5–150 μM) with a detection limit of 55.8 nM. It is important to mention here that an evident change in fluorescence color from green to blue has been observed with the addition of F- ion to the NCDs/morin-Al3+ system which can be used as a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform for F- detection. The detection limit for this visual sensing has been calculated to be 2.09 μM. Authors were encouraged to apply this platform for the detection of F- in different water samples with pleasing results. Thus this probe is found to be act as a smartphone-assisted visual detection as well as ratiometric optical detection of F- ion (Fig. 5). According to the authors it can have broad application vision in environmental field.

Based on the nonradiative electron transfer from CDs to metal ions and reversible binding between the CDs and metal ions, Li et al. designed a fluorescent quenching receptor with less biotoxicity [61]. The receptor was incorporated on the surface of mesoporous superparamagnetic Fe3O4@mSiO2 nanoparticle which resulted a system Fe3O4@mSiO2-SiCDs@DTPA-Mn+/NCDs. This probe has been found to be functioned both as F- detection as well as removal. In presence of F- ion the NCDs are found to be detached and original fluorescence of NCDs is recovered. A soft-soft interaction between metal ions and carboxyl group of NCDs causes nonradiative electron transfer from NCDs to metal ions. The limit of detection has been calculated to be 65 nM with a linear range of 1 to 25 μM. The novelty of this probe is its recyclable performance. Removal capacity of F- could be up to 21.4 mg/g.

Bimodal excitatoion behavior of N doped CDs has been reported by Guo et al. for the ratiometric detection of fluoride ions [62]. NCDs were synthesized by using a mixture of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and melamine by hydrothermal process. NCDs/curcumin complex has been constructed by mixing NCDs and curcumin in a buffer solution of pH 9. Several inorganic ions including cations and anions are introduced into the probe to detect the selectivity. It has been seen that only F- can cause a distinct change in fluorescence intensity. A highly sensitive response towards F- is observed with a linear range of 0–50 μM F- ion concentration. The detection limit is calculated to be 57 nM. Fluorescence intensity of NCDs is found to be decreased by curcumin due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Addition of F- generates curcumin-F- adduct. Thus localization of inner filter effect was seen to occur which result an overlap between the absorption of curcumin-F- complex and excitation of NCDs leading to the inverse variation in fluorescence signals. Interesting result inspired the authors to develop a smartphone integrated platform for visual detection of F- ion in real water and food samples.

Based on carbon dots/gold nanoparticles hybrid system Feng et al. fabricated a logic gate sensor for the selective detection of fluoride anion [63]. Boronic acid decorated CDs were synthesizesd by using 3-aminophenylboronic acid. 3-mercapto-1,2-propanediol (MP) was mixed with the gold nanoparticles to obtain MP modified Au NPs (MP-Au NPs). Finally, b-CDs@MP-Au NPs nanocomposites were fabricated by mixing b-CDs solution with MP-Au NPs. In this system gold nanoparticles are found to act as energy acceptor, whereas b-CDs are found to function as a energy donor by using MP as a bridge. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from donor to acceptor, fluorescent signal is turning off. Interestingly, when F- is introduced into the system, it has been observed to bind with the b-CDs from the composite and inhibit the FRET process, thereby activate the quenched photoluminescence of the probe. The linearity for F- detection is observed from 9 μM to 117 μM with the limit of detection of 1.5 μM. Impressive outcome encouraged the authors to develop the molecular AND logic gate for the practical application where anions or metal ions can be used as chemical inputs. Moreover, this method is also applied to detect F- ion in real samples.

Mohapatra et al. synthesized a B, N, S doped CDs by using 3-amino phenyl boronic acid, citric acid and thiourea as precursors. These CDs were integrated with dopamine to develop a fluorescence turn on sensing probe which can detect F- ion selectively and sensitively in purely aqueous solution [64]. Different anions have been tested with the developed probe but it has found that the system is highly selective towards F- ions. Photoelectron transfer (PET) from BNS-CDs to dopamine decreases the intense fluorescence of the CDs. However, in presence of F- the PL intensity of CDs-dopamine probe found to be increased significantly. This can be explained by taking into the account that the central boron atom forms a trifluoroboron complex upon binding with F- ions and restrict the PET, which causes enhancement of PL intensity. On the other hand boronic acid functionalized BNS-CDs have a large π-conjugated system. Addition of F- to this π-conjugated probe can form a p-π conjugation which helps to increase the electron density on HOMO. Thus more electrons are jumped from HOMO to LUMO upon excitation and resulting in an enhancement in fluorescence. LOD of the probe has been observed as 0.7pM. The cytotoxicity study has revealed that the probe is not cytotoxic and can be used to sense F- ion in human serum as well as to monitor F- contamination inside the living cell.

Hou et al. reported [65] a yellowish-green emissive NCDs synthesized by solvent-free method with the use of citric acid and dicyandiamide as starting materials. Direct addition of F- to the NCDs does not show any significant effect. Fe3+ is seen to quench the fluorescence intensity of NCDs and interestingly, when F- has been added to the NCDs-Fe3+ system, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs is recovered. Fe3+ quenches the PL of NCDs but addition of F- restores the fluorescence of NCDs. Different metal ions and anions like Cl-, Br- I-, SO42-, NO3 -, PO4 3-, CO3 2- and CH3COO- are introduced to study the specificity of sensing. The system is seen to be selective on F- ion. According to the authors, stable ionic compound (FeF3) is formed upon addition of F- to the NCDs-Fe3+ system. High lattice energy of the compound makes it stable in aqueous media. Thus, Fe3+ can be removed from the NCDs surface to make NCDs free to recover its fluorescence. The limit of detection is calculated to be 75 nmol with a linear range of 0.2–60 µM of F- ion concentration. Interestingly, the probe is found to be applicable for detecting F- in real water samples also.

An environment friendly probe for F- ions was reported by Kainth et al [67]. Natural ingredients (e.g. mosambi peels (Citrus limetta), l-ascorbic acid and urea) were used to synthesize oxygen and nitrogen rich CDs by microwave method and named as OCDs and NCDs. To investigate the mechanism of the sensing probes, firstly different cations have been introduced to the both types of CDs solutions. Fluorescence result suggests that both CDs are selective for only Fe3+. Authors have proposed that a FRET is to be occured between CDs and Fe3+. After that both the OCD/NCD-Fe3+ systems are tested for the detection of F- ions and it has been observed that only OCD-Fe3+ mixture is selective for F- ions (Fig. 8 ). Limit of detection for this probe is calculated to be 0.01 mM which is lower than the value defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (41.5 ppm). Impressive results encouraged the authors to develop various molecular logic gates, e.g. NOT, IMP, AND and OR, by the use of metal ions and anions as the chemical inputs (Fig. 8). Sensing capacity of the probe is also investigated in real samples (lake and tap water). Authors have demanded that the probe may be beneficial in molecular electronic and nano-devices as well as in the real-time detection of F- ions.

Smart, portable and user friendly microdevices are the current attraction round the globe in all kinds of materials. Liu et al. reported such type of ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (AA-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+)-based smartphone sensing platform for intelligent fluoride ion detection [68]. Ascorbic acid derived carbon dots (AA-CDs) were combined with Al3+, where aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIE) has been observed and designed as the response signal. On the other hand ruthenium bipyridine molecule [Ru(bpy)3]2+ is selected as the reference signal. In presence of various concentration of F −, fluorescence is found to be changed from red to cyan. A vivid change in fluorescence color from red to cyan with the continuous addition of F- can be visually distinguished with the help of naked eye also. This interesting result encouraged the authors to develop a smartphone color recognition APP i.e. visual quantitative detection through red-green-blue (RGB) channel values of the extracted fluorescence image. A detection limit for F- has been calculated to be 1.53 μM, which is far below the concentration of F- in drinking water set by the WHO. The fascination of the current probe implies not only its portability, cost friendly, easy operation, good stability and selectivity but also its rapid detection, satisfactory repeatability. Thus it is applied on many real samples like water and toothpaste etc. Authors also demanded that the probe can be a promising platform in many fields like food safety assessment, environmental protection and diagnosis.

Li et al. developed a ratiometric nanosensor for the selective detection and removal of F- ions [69]. They synthesized pyrene-based boronic acid fluorescent probe PyB which shows poor solubility in water. CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of Carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CCNC). To increase the solubility and sensitivity for the detection of F-, CDs-PyB composite has been made by immobilizing PyB on the surface of CDs in 80 % THF solution to fabricate a fluorescene resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. Sensing mechanism of the probe has been investigated and found that upon excitation of 355 nm, PyB exhibits emission at 381 nm, which can be absorbed by the CDs. Thus dual emissions (381 nm and 460 nm) are observed for CDs-PyB upon excitation at 355 nm. Enhancement of fluorescence in pyrene moiety is seen by the addition of F-. Interaction of the charged boronate and amide groups causes short distance between CDs and the pyrene facilitating energy transfer between PyB and the CDs. This phenomenon results an increase of the fluorescence signal at 460 nm and reduction of the fluorescence at 381 nm. A linear response for F- concentrations has been observed over a range from 0 to 200 μM (R2 = 0.996). The limit of detection is calculated to be 5.9 × 10–5 M. Results with this probe prompted them further to investigate the detection and removal potential of amino modified cellulose membrane based platform containing CDs-PyB (CPC membrane), which exhibit very good detection and extraction capacity for F- from wastewater. An electrostatic interaction between F- and the membrane may be occurred to remove the anions. In brief, -NH2 group in the membrane can be protonated in aqueous medium and thereby formed -NH3 +. A hydrogen bonding and ionic interaction between F- ion and -NH3 + can be the reason for adsorption of F- and removal of it. Moreover, this CPC membrane is found to be excellently biocompatible.

Baruah et al. constructed an interesting probe for the selective and sensitive detection of fluoride ions in water [70]. Here, CDs were synthesized from chitosan gel and capped with cyclodextrin (CDs/β-cd) and calix[4]arene-25,26,27,28-tetrol (CDs/C[4]A). While capping with the cyclodextrin and calix[4]arene-25,26,27,28-tetrol the fluorescence intensity of CDs is found to be quenched. Surprisingly, when F- ions are introduced to the probe an enhancement as well as restoration in PL is observed. Emission intensities of capped CDs are found to be quenched. This may be attributed to non-radiative recombination pathway. When F- is added, it is assumed to enter into the cavity of β-cd and C[4]A to form β-cd-F- and C[4]A-F-, which results in the release of CDs, and thereby enhancement in fluorescence intensity. This system is also selective towards F −ion, which is evident by checking with other halide ions i.e. Cl-, Br- and I- and hydroxyl ion (OH-), when these ions are introduced fluorescence intensities are seen to be decreased. Limit of detection with this present probe has been estimated to be ∼6.6 µM. According to the author, fluorescence based sensor can be developed especially for anions by introducing this process.

Unique yellow emissive water soluble CDs have been prepared by using cetylpyridinium chloride (CPC) surfactant as a precursor by an easy single step microwave synthesis [71]. An interesting feature has been observed during the photoluminescence study of the CDs when Fe2+ and Fe3+ are introduced into the CDs solution. The PL quenching of the CDs is different with the different oxidation states of iron. Both the combined systems viz. CDs@Fen+ (n = 2 and 3) are used to monitor the quantification of F- ions. It has been observed that CDs@Fe3+ system shows more efficient recovery of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in presence of F- ions, whereas CDs@Fe2+ nanoprobe is found to be inactive towards fluoride ions. Detection limit is calculated to be 1.26 mM. To get further insight time-resolved fluorescence decays are measured for CDs and CDs-Fen+ system in presence of F- ions. Similar result has been observed, i.e. CDs@Fe3+ system shows a good recovery in a lifetime in presence of F- ions. All these findings demand that the surfactant derived CDs can be useful for the qualitative and quantitative detection of multiple analytes through turn-off-on strategy.

A novel fluorescent probe Zr(CDs-COO)2EDTA was designed based on the competitive ligand exchange reaction between -COOH group and F- ion on the CDs surface [72]. Several anions are introduced to check the selectivity of the probe and it has been observed that the probe is very selective towards F- ions. The strong fluorescence emission intensity of the probe is found to be quenched by the addition of F- ions. This can be explained by taking into the fact that a non-fluorescent complex Zr(F)2EDTA is formed by the ligand exchange reaction. Stronger affinity of F- ion to Zr(IV) over -COOH group is responsible for the formation of fluorinated complex from the probe. On-off mechanism is proposed for this highly selective detection of F- by this probe. A good linear relationship (0.10 µM to 10 µM) is observed for the probe with respect to the F- content. Most importantly, fluoride detection in real samples like water samples and toothpaste has also observed with satisfactory results.

A combined theoretical as well as experimental study have been done on a water soluble fluoride recognition probe by Mohammadpour et al. in which CDs were used as the fluorophores and arylboronic acids as the absorbers [73]. Tuning of absorption profiles toward shorter and longer wavelengths is carried out by the reaction of F- with the boron center of arylboronic acids. When spectral overlap between absorber and fluorophore is in higher extent then a drop off fluorescence intensity of CDs is observed, which is indicative of quantitative fluoride measurement. This occurs due to the IFE. The lower limit of detection of F- by this probe satisfies the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) requirement for water safety. Additionally, DFT calculations have also done to find out the electronic structures and optical absorption spectra of arylboronic acids without and with the addition of F- ions. An excellent similarity in the result i.e. forecast of spectral change is observed between experimental and theoretical results. IFE effect between the CDs and arylboronic acids is found to be decreased. Thus the probe may be very useful for the recognition of F- in aqueous media.

Present study illustrates that detection of F- ion by only CDs is very rare as the photoluminescence of the CDs cannot be altered that much by the direct addition of F- to CDs solution. Thus, to make CDs-based fluorescence sensor for F-, CDs are attached to the metal ions like Al3+, Fe3+, Eu3+to make composite system which can perform through on-off-on fluorescence strategy to sense F-. Here, metal ions quenches the PL of the CDs and when F- is added to these composite system metal ions detach from the CDs and combine to F-. Fluoride ion being a hard base can easily combine to these hard acids (Al3+, Fe3+, Eu3+) to form stable complexes. Thus, PL of the CDs regenerates. Some other systems have been found to develop by combining the CDs to organic molecules/coordination complexes/nanoparticles which can function through various fluorescence mechanisms like IFE, AIEE, FRET, PET etc. Thus, this report provides an idea about making the composite systems by using the CDs which may be helpful for the researchers to develop new and highly effective sensing systems for F-.

In summary, we have presented an overview of the recent reports on selective and sensitive detection of fluoride ions by carbon dots-based fluorescent probes in aqueous media and environmental samples. CDs being a water soluble, eco-friendly, cost effective and biocompatible probe have been massively used for the sensing of various important bio-analytes. It has been observed that direct sensing of fluoride ions by only CDs is very rare in literature. Thus researchers have developed such hybrid/composite systems, where basic component is carbon dots and these hybrid systems can selectively and sensitively detect F- in aqueous media and in real samples. Mechanistic study reveals that these probes can function with different fluorescence methods like on-off-on, inner filter effect, aggregation induced emission, fluorescence resonance energy transfer etc. In some cases colorimetric probes have also been developed which can detect the presence of F- in the samples through naked eye. Therefore, the uses of CDs based green systems as F- ion sensors in aqueous media are highly appreciated. Most interestingly, several ready-to-use systems and mobile application (APP)/smartphone assisted systems have also been developed for F- ions sensing, which are user friendly, portable and very much attractive. In this review mechanistic discussion on the fluorimetric detection of F- ions have been summarized. Hopefully this article will help the researchers to gain more and more interest in discovering new sensing probes for different important bioanalytes in water media and environmental systems.
